visual_checks: Check the PDF bookmarks panel to see if bookmarks are present
Count the total number of pages in the document to determine if bookmarks are expected (documents over 9 pages should have bookmarks)
Compare the bookmark structure against the heading structure visible in the document content
Verify that bookmark labels match or closely correspond to the heading text in the document
Check that the bookmark hierarchy reflects the heading levels (H1, H2, H3) used in the document
pass_conditions: PDFs with more than 9 pages include bookmarks in the bookmarks panel
Bookmarks correspond to the heading structure of the document
Bookmark labels match the heading text they point to
Bookmark nesting/hierarchy reflects the document's heading level hierarchy
Each bookmark navigates to the correct location in the document
fail_conditions: A PDF with more than 9 pages has no bookmarks at all
Bookmarks are present but do not match the document's heading structure
Bookmark labels are generic or auto-generated (e.g., 'Bookmark 1', 'Page 3') rather than matching headings
Bookmarks point to incorrect locations in the document
The bookmark hierarchy is flat when the document has multiple heading levels
Significant sections or headings in the document are missing from the bookmarks
na_conditions: The PDF is 9 pages or fewer and bookmarks are not required
The document is not a PDF
ne_conditions: The PDF bookmark data could not be extracted for evaluation
The total page count of the document could not be determined
common_mistakes: Converting a Word document to PDF without enabling the option to generate bookmarks from headings
Having bookmarks that were correct for an earlier version of the document but were not updated after content changes
Creating bookmarks only for top-level headings while omitting sub-section headings that provide important navigation
Bookmark text not matching the actual heading text in the document due to manual editing
Generating bookmarks from a table of contents rather than the heading structure, resulting in mismatches

examples.pass: A 45-page policy document has bookmarks for every heading (H1 through H3) that match the document's heading text and navigate to the correct sections
examples.fail: A 30-page annual report has no bookmarks, forcing assistive technology users to navigate page by page with no way to jump to specific sections
examples.partial: A long PDF has bookmarks for the main chapter headings but is missing bookmarks for all sub-section headings, limiting navigation granularity
